methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $700M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 8 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $120M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Amogy, a Series C climate and energy company founded by Seonghoon Woo, is evaluated against a peer set of eight private companies in the same sector and stage. The comparable universe spans valuation scales from $120M to $6.0B, with notable peers including KoBold Metals ($2.0B), Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B), and Redwood Materials ($6.0B at Series D). One public comparable is included in the analysis.

Two valuation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier to Amogy's last post-money valuation of $700M, implying $1.2B. The peer-median method anchors to the median valuation of eight same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables, which stands at $450M. This reflects meaningful dispersion in the peer set: the median sits substantially below Amogy's current $700M valuation, though several peers (KoBold, Electric Hydrogen) command notably higher valuations at the same stage.

Triangulating both methods produces a range of $382M to $1.4B, with a median of $820M. The current $700M valuation sits in the lower-middle portion of this range. Key uncertainties include whether Amogy's technology differentiation and capital efficiency justify a premium to the peer median of $450M, and whether the stage-progression multiplier of 1.7x appropriately captures next-stage dynamics in a heterogeneous climate-tech sector. The wide dispersion in peer valuations ($120M–$6.0B) underscores valuation sensitivity to technology maturity and go-to-market progress.
